🟥 THE SUITCASE SCANDAL: DISSECTING THE TIMING AND THE PLAYERS BEHIND THE P805-BILLION BRIBERY CLAIMS

[An MCT Opinion]

I've been following Philippine politics long enough to recognize a coordinated counter-attack when I see one. 

And what unfolded on February 23, 2026, at Club Filipino in San Juan was textbook political warfare—the kind that makes you sit up, grab your notebook, and start connecting dots.

Before you go any further, know that this is a personal reading of events and motivations, not a formal statement of facts or a legal conclusion.

Let me walk you through what happened, who's behind it, and why the timing matters more than the actual allegations.

🟥 THE BOMBSHELL THAT DROPPED ON OPENING DAY

February 23, 2026. As the ICC opened the confirmation of charges hearing against Rodrigo Duterte in The Hague, a lawyer named Levito “Levi” Baligod was holding a press conference back in Manila. With him: 18 men claiming to be former Philippine Marines who allegedly worked as bagmen delivering cash in suitcases.

The claims were staggering. We're talking P805 billion in cash supposedly collected from the Valle Verde residence of fugitive former congressman Elizaldy "Zaldy" Co and distributed to politicians including President Marcos Jr. and former House Speaker Martin Romualdez.

But the real bomb? They claimed P56 million (roughly $2 million) was delivered to ICC investigators when they visited the Philippines in December 2023. According to these supposed marines, the ICC team was "wined and dined" by former senator Antonio Trillanes IV and his group.

Same day. Same hour. The ICC hearing begins in The Hague, and suddenly there's a massive bribery scandal in Manila targeting the very people who helped build the case against Duterte.

If you don't find that timing suspicious, you haven't been paying attention.

🟥 WHO IS LEVI BALIGOD?

Baligod isn't some random lawyer. 

He made his name representing whistleblowers during the 2013 PDAF scandal—the pork barrel scam that brought down Janet Lim-Napoles and several lawmakers. He was the face of anti-corruption advocacy for a hot minute.

But here's what people forget: based on reports, his own whistleblowers dropped him. 

Benhur Luy and Merlina Suñas terminated his services in 2014, complaining he was too busy with "other advocacies." 

That's not exactly a ringing endorsement from people whose credibility he helped establish.

Fast forward to 2026, and Baligod is running for representative of Leyte's 5th District. He needs publicity, he needs a platform, and he's found both by aligning himself with the Duterte camp's narrative.

🟥 THE 18 "MARINES" WHO WEREN'T

The Philippine Navy moved fast. Within 24 hours, they debunked the credentials of these supposed former Marines. Four of the 18 were never in the military at all. Most of the others? Dishonorably discharged.

Senator Panfilo Lacson listed the problems with their story:

- They claimed to be associates of Master Sgt. Orly Guteza, a figure from Senate investigations into flood control corruption  
- Their timelines don't match military records  
- Their rank designations are inconsistent  
- No credible chain of command to justify cash deliveries of this scale  

But Baligod presented them with all the pageantry of legitimate whistleblowers. Photos with suitcases. Detailed accounts of deliveries. The whole production.

🟥 ZALDY CO: THE FUGITIVE AT THE CENTER

To understand this story, you need to know who Elizaldy "Zaldy" Co is and why his name carries weight.

Co was a congressman representing the Ako Bicol party-list. His company, Sunwest Construction, became the center of investigations into ghost flood control projects worth hundreds of billions of pesos. The Sandiganbayan declared him a fugitive from justice when he fled the country in 2022.

He's now believed to be in Portugal, using a Portuguese passport to avoid extradition. The Valle Verde residence mentioned in the allegations? That's Co's property.

So there's real corruption here—that part isn't fabricated. As reported, Co is a fugitive connected to massive flood control scams. 

What Baligod did was clever: he took a real corruption scandal (Zaldy Co's flood control projects) and grafted it onto the ICC investigation, mixing truth with fiction to create something that sounds plausible.

🟥 WHO'S AMPLIFYING THIS STORY?

Look at who's pushing this script and you'll understand the political agenda:

Mike Defensor - Former congressman, hardcore Duterte ally, known critic of the Marcos administration. He's been on every talk show that will have him, hammering the bribery angle.

Jay Sonza - Former broadcaster who ran for vice president in 2010 under Kilusang Bagong Lipunan (Marcos Sr.'s old party). He's a longtime Duterte supporter who's been using social media to spread the allegations.

Imee Marcos – Senator and presidential sister who has jumped in by calling for a Senate probe into the 18 men’s affidavit, effectively giving the suitcase storyline official backing and fresh media oxygen.

Baligod himself - Now a political candidate who needs name recognition and positioning.

These aren't neutral observers doing investigative journalism. These are political operators with clear loyalties pushing a spin that serves very specific interests.

Leila de Lima—who’s been on the receiving end of Duterte’s persecution and knows many of these Duterte-aligned operators well—once described figures like these as “dubious, discredited personalities.” That’s her assessment of the Duterte camp’s usual messengers, not a blanket label for everyone now circling this story.

🟥 WHY TRILLANES? BECAUSE HE'S THE ARCHITECT

Antonio Trillanes IV is the one person the Duterte camp hates more than anyone else right now. The former senator, former military officer who led coup attempts against Arroyo, became Duterte's most persistent and vocal critic.

And he didn't just criticize from the sidelines. Trillanes publicly acknowledged helping the ICC investigation by facilitating contact with witnesses. He helped coordinate testimonies. He helped build the case that now has Duterte sitting in The Hague.

From the Duterte camp's perspective, Trillanes is the architect of their downfall. So of course they're targeting him. 

If they can discredit Trillanes, they can discredit the entire network of witnesses and evidence that fed into the ICC investigation.

Trillanes has denied everything and is filing cyberlibel cases against Baligod, Defensor, Sonza, the 18 supposed marines, and anyone else spreading the allegations. He's not backing down.

🟥 THE MARCOS ANGLE: LEVERAGE AND THREATS

Here's where it gets really interesting. The allegations don't just target the ICC and Trillanes—they also claim President Marcos Jr. is the "mastermind" behind the P805 billion scheme.

Why would the Duterte camp implicate their former ally?

Because the Marcos-Duterte alliance is dead. The "UniTeam" that won the 2022 elections collapsed over:

- Confidential funds controversy with VP Sara Duterte  
- ICC cooperation—Marcos allowed Duterte's arrest in March 2025  
- West Philippine Sea policy differences  
- 2028 presidential positioning  

Sara Duterte is facing impeachment with a trial supposedly set for June 2026. Her father is in The Hague facing crimes against humanity charges. The Duterte political dynasty is fighting for its survival.

By implicating Marcos in a massive corruption scandal, the Duterte camp appears to be sending a message: "We can drag you down with us. Back off, or we'll make this hurt."

It looks like political hostage-taking disguised as whistleblowing.

🟥 WILL THIS IMPACT THE ICC CASE?

Short answer: Probably not in any meaningful way.

The ICC operates under strict legal standards. The judges in The Hague evaluate evidence according to international criminal law, not Filipino social media trends. The prosecution has presented systematic evidence:

- Operation Double Barrel documentation  
- Death lists from Davao Death Squad operations  
- Witness testimonies from former police officers  
- Thousands of documented killings with consistent patterns  

Former Supreme Court Justice Antonio Carpio, who's been monitoring the proceedings, confirmed the prosecution clearly demonstrated "systemic and widespread" killings. That's the threshold for crimes against humanity.

The ICC also has rigorous protocols for investigating allegations of misconduct. 

If there were credible evidence of bribery, they would investigate it separately. 

But transparently timed allegations from debunked "marines" presented by political operators? That's not going to derail a years-long investigation.

However, domestically, it will:

- Create political noise that affects public opinion in the Philippines  
- Give Duterte supporters a counter-narrative  
- Complicate Sara Duterte's impeachment proceedings  
- Test how far Marcos is willing to go in supporting ICC cooperation  

🟥 WHAT THIS REALLY TELLS US

Strip away the suitcases, the fake marines, the absurd peso amounts, and what you have is this: what appears to be a coordinated propaganda offensive by a political dynasty watching their patriarch face international justice.

They're using every tool in the playbook:

- Discrediting key critics (Trillanes)  
- Attacking the legitimacy of the legal process (ICC bribery claims)  
- Threatening former allies (implicating Marcos)  
- Presenting witnesses whose credentials were officially debunked  
- Timing the attack for maximum disruption (same day as ICC hearing)  

This is how political families fight when they're cornered. 

They don't defend themselves by proving innocence—they attack the process, the witnesses, and the investigators. 

They create chaos that makes people throw up their hands and say, "Everyone's dirty anyway."

But the evidence presented in The Hague doesn't depend on Trillanes's reputation or Baligod's press conferences. 

It depends on documented patterns of killings, witness testimonies under oath, and systemic policy directives. That's a much harder thing to discredit with suitcases full of fake cash.

🟥 THE QUESTION YOU SHOULD BE ASKING

If you're the Duterte camp and you genuinely have evidence of ICC corruption, why would you:

- Present it through debunked "marines" instead of credible witnesses?  
- Use political operators with clear loyalties instead of neutral investigators?  
- Time it for the exact day of the ICC hearing instead of presenting it to proper authorities beforehand?  
- Make claims so absurdly large (P805 billion) that they strain credibility?  

The play is obvious: throw out wild, well-timed accusations so people end up talking about the circus in Manila instead of the evidence being laid out in The Hague.

And for researchers, bloggers, and citizens trying to understand what's really happening, the timing, the players, and the tactics tell you everything you need to know.

Strip away the stunt with the 18 men and their suitcases, and you’re left with a clear pattern: a political dynasty under pressure reaching for every available weapon—questionable claims, pressure tactics, and already debunked allegations—to blunt the impact of thousands of documented deaths.

That's the story. Everything else is just noise.

Forty years.

I didn't march on EDSA in 1986. I was too young, standing behind a plastic table in Marikina, selling samalamig while the grown-ups decided the country’s future. The turo-turo beside my small stall had its radio turned all the way up, EDSA coverage spilling into the street, and people kept gathering there, forks mid-air, listening for every bulletin, every rumor, every breathless update.

I wasn't there when nuns handed flowers to soldiers, when Radio Veritas became a lifeline, or when a dictator's helicopter lifted off the Palace lawn and disappeared into history.

But I live with what EDSA left behind.

I live with the contradictions, the unfinished sentences, the half-kept promises. I live in a country where, under a Marcos presidency, February 25 was quietly downgraded in the calendar. I live with textbooks that keep changing, relatives who don't agree on what happened, and a generation of young people who first learned about People Power from a multiple-choice exam or a TikTok video that called Martial Law a golden age.

Today is the 40th anniversary of the People Power Revolution. And I don't know what to feel.

I want to believe EDSA meant something. I want to believe the courage of those who stood on that highway still echoes in the halls of government, in classrooms, in the choices we make at the ballot box.

But the weight of what we're losing presses harder every year.

🟥 THE RESET THAT NEVER REALLY RESET

EDSA was supposed to be our system reboot. The moment we purged the dictator, dismantled the machinery of tyranny, and started fresh.

Except we didn't start fresh.

We changed the container, but the contents remained. The patronage networks, the political dynasties, the same families who fed off the system before 1972—they adapted, survived, and came back stronger.

The Marcoses themselves were allowed to return in 1991. By 1992, Bongbong Marcos was already in Congress. By 2022, he was president.

In 1986, Radio Veritas and the mosquito press broke through censorship to carry the truth to ordinary Filipinos. Decades later, social media platforms and their recommendation systems often do the opposite, amplifying paid propaganda and historical distortion.

Forty years after we toppled a dictator, his son sits in the Palace, social media influencers rewrite Martial Law as a period of peace and prosperity, and a generation of voters—shaped by YouTube montages and curated nostalgia—believes the myth more than the testimony of survivors.

This is what haunts me most. EDSA removed Marcos from power, but we never finished the work. We never fully dismantled the structures that allowed him to rule. We never held the enablers accountable. We never built the institutions strong enough to prevent it from happening again.

And so here we are. The code wasn't deleted. It was just running in the background, waiting for the right conditions to execute again.

🟥 AN INHERITANCE WITHOUT A MANUAL

I didn't witness EDSA. But I inherited its consequences.

I inherited a democracy that looks functional on paper but bends under the weight of dynasties and corruption.

I inherited a media landscape where truth competes with paid influencers, sponsored content, and coordinated disinformation campaigns designed to make you doubt what you see with your own eyes.

I inherited a country where survivors of torture sit next to revisionist politicians in the Senate, where whistleblowers are harassed and persecuted, and where the grandchildren of victims now scroll past memes calling their lolos' suffering “fake news.”

What do you do with a revolution you only know through other people's memories?

What do you do when those memories are contradicted by your relatives, your teachers, your timeline, and the influencers being paid to rewrite history?

The Catholic educators are calling EDSA an “unfinished movement.” They say it demands vigilance, critical thinking, and resistance to revisionism.

But most of us are just trying to survive. We're scrolling, working, commuting, paying bills. February 25 is a working day now. We clock in, check emails, and watch from the sidelines as history gets rewritten in real time.

And I worry that we're forgetting how to recognize the warning signs.

🟥 FROM RADIO VERITAS TO TROLL FARMS

There's a brutal irony in how we got here.

In 1986, the “mosquito press” and Radio Veritas broke through censorship. Independent media, underground newsletters, and church networks became the instruments of truth when the dictatorship controlled everything else.

Forty years later, we have the most powerful information technology in human history. And we're using it to spread lies faster than truth can catch up.

Troll farms. Coordinated disinformation. Influencers paid to sanitize Martial Law. Cambridge Analytica-style operations that turned nostalgia into political capital and the Marcos name into a landslide victory.

We once needed courage to photocopy the truth. Now you need courage just to post it. Because the moment you do, the trolls descend, the harassment begins, and your inbox fills with rape threats, death threats, and red-tagging.

I've watched this happen to journalists, to fact-checkers, to historians who dare to speak about Martial Law victims. I've watched it happen to people like Jun Lozada, who risked everything to expose corruption and spent years fighting legal persecution for telling the truth.

And I've watched it happen to ordinary people who just wanted to share what they learned, what they researched, what their grandparents told them. They get drowned out by coordinated mass reporting, brigading, and cancel campaigns designed to silence dissent.

The machinery of disinformation didn't just help elect a Marcos. It rewrote the entire EDSA narrative. It turned the victims into liars, the resisters into communists, and the dictator into a misunderstood hero.

And now, forty years after the People Power Revolution, we're living in a country where truth is treated as just another brand of propaganda.

🟥 THE FANTASY OF ANOTHER RESET

Some people say we should do it again. Tear it all down. Reset the government. Start over.

I understand the frustration. I feel it too. The corruption never stopped. The dynasties never left. The institutions remain weak, captured by the same elite families who benefit from keeping them that way.

But here's what terrifies me. System collapse doesn't punish the powerful first. It crushes the poor.

EDSA was a near-collapse. We came dangerously close to the edge in 1986. By the grace of soldiers who refused to shoot and a people who refused to back down, we survived without a bloodbath.

But collapse is not a strategy. And anyone who romanticizes burning it all down has never lived through what happens when the state disappears, when law breaks down, when warlords and gangsters fill the vacuum left by government.

EDSA showed us that removing a dictator is the beginning of responsibility, not the end of it.

The work that comes after—the slow, boring, unglamorous work of building institutions, reforming systems, holding people accountable, teaching the next generation—that's the part we never finished.

And so the same families came back. The same corruption adapted. The same impunity returned.

🟥 WHAT “FOR MY COUNTRY” REALLY MEANS

At EDSA, people said “I did it for my country” and meant it. They risked their lives, not knowing if soldiers would shoot or tanks would roll.

But that same line—“I did it for my country”—has been used to justify torture, extrajudicial killings, plunder, and the erosion of every institution EDSA tried to protect.

Duterte said it when he launched a drug war that killed thousands.

Politicians say it when they pocket public funds, when they protect their dynasties, when they red-tag activists and silence critics.

Even now, as the Marcos-Duterte alliance fractures and both families accuse each other of destabilization, they each claim the moral high ground. They each say they're defending democracy.

Meanwhile, the institutions they were supposed to serve—Congress, the courts, the agencies meant to hold them accountable—bend and stretch to protect the powerful.

What does “for my country” really mean when the people saying it are the ones bleeding the country dry?

And what does it mean for those of us who watch, exhausted and angry, but still trying to figure out what we're supposed to do about it?

🟥 THE QUESTION I'M AFRAID TO ANSWER

I think about this often now. What will I tell my child—or anyone's child—about EDSA?

Will I tell them it was a victory? A warning? An unfinished assignment?

Will I tell them about the courage of the people who marched, knowing they could die?

Will I tell them about the survivors who still carry the scars of torture, who watched their families mourn, who spent decades fighting for justice only to see a Marcos son elected president?

Will I tell them about the troll farms, the influencers, the disinformation networks that turned Martial Law into nostalgia content?

Will I tell them about the dynasties that never left, the corruption that never stopped, the institutions that never became strong enough to protect us?

Or will I tell them the truth?

That EDSA was both a miracle and a warning. That we removed a dictator but never finished dismantling the system that allowed him to rule. That we won the streets in 1986 and lost the algorithm in the 2010s. That democracy is not a moment but a daily decision—and we keep choosing comfort over vigilance, nostalgia over truth, silence over resistance.

🟥 HOPING AGAINST HOPE

I don't know how to end this. I don't have a call to action that feels honest. I don't have a vision of hope that doesn't sound hollow.

I'm tired. We're all tired.

But I still write. I still research. I still try to connect the dots, even when the trolls are loud and the odds feel impossible.

Because EDSA taught us one thing that still matters: that ordinary people, when they finally decide they've had enough, can change the course of history.

The question is whether we'll decide in time.

Forty years after EDSA, I'm praying we will. I'm hoping against hope that the next generation won't inherit a country where truth is optional, where justice is for sale, and where memory itself has been sold to the highest bidder.

EDSA is not over. It's unfinished. And whether it becomes a victory or a cautionary tale depends on what we do—today, tomorrow, and every day after.

I pray we choose well. Amen.

🟥 THE LAST BOAT AND THE ISLAND OF SCREAMS

[An MCT Reflection]

Joel Butuyan stood before the International Criminal Court judges last Monday, supposedly addressing them in legal language about confirming charges against Rodrigo Duterte. 

But if you listened closely—really listened—his opening statement was less a courtroom argument and more a warning shot across the bow for every Filipino back home.

He talked about a boat. The victims' "last boat" to justice, he called it. 

If the ICC doesn't confirm the charges, he said, these families will be "forever moored on an island where the nights are filled with the screams and cries of their massacred loved ones."

That image should haunt us. 

Because Butuyan wasn't just painting a picture for Dutch judges in The Hague. He was holding up a mirror to an entire nation that has learned to scroll past body counts like they're weather reports.

🟥 THE VIRUS METAPHOR WE NEED TO HEAR

Here's where Butuyan got uncomfortably direct. He called impunity a virus. Not just any virus—one that Duterte spread, that created "clones" of himself, that converted "millions of peace-loving citizens into bloodthirsty disciples who have become converts to the belief that violence and killings are valid solutions to societal problems."

Read that again. He's talking about us. 

About how we cheered when Duterte promised to "fatten the fish in Manila Bay" with criminals. 

About how we posted "less criminals, less crimes" on Facebook when the body count hit triple digits. 

About how we convinced ourselves that extrajudicial killings were acceptable as long as they happened to "other people's" sons and brothers and fathers.

Butuyan's prescription? "Truth is the antidote to the virus of impunity." 

But here's the problem—you can't cure people who don't think they're sick.

🟥 THE CONQUERING HERO SCENARIO

Butuyan warned the judges that if charges aren't confirmed, Duterte returns home "as a conquering hero" who "will resume preaching his gospel of impunity." 

The lawyer pointed out something that should terrify everyone: if Duterte could threaten to slap ICC judges while in office, imagine what message that sends to victims living in communities "that thrive" on support for the former president. 

Many families are afraid to even participate in the ICC case because they're surrounded by neighbors who still worship Duterte, who still believe the drug war was justified, who might turn violent against anyone seeking accountability.

This is the Philippines Duterte left behind. Not safer. Not cleaner. Just more afraid. More divided. More willing to accept violence as normal.

🟥 THE NUREMBERG COMPARISON

Near the end of his statement, Butuyan made a historical comparison that should make every Filipino sit up straight. 

He said this trial needs to create "a historical account on the mass murder of so many Filipinos during the Duterte regime" that future generations can access "just like the Nuremberg trials and the Tokyo trials so that they will learn from it and not repeat the same mistake of selecting and glorifying leaders who preach and practice impunity."

Think about that. We're being compared to Nazi Germany and Imperial Japan. Not by some foreign critic with an agenda, but by a Filipino lawyer representing Filipino victims before an international court. 

The drug war killed at least 6,000 by official counts, over 30,000 according to human rights groups. 

Those numbers demand a reckoning on par with history's worst atrocities.

But here's the uncomfortable truth: many Filipinos still don't see it that way. 

They see Duterte as the man who "cleaned up" the streets, who "had the balls" to do what needed doing. 

Butuyan's statement was an attempt to break through that delusion, to force us to confront what we allowed to happen in our name.

🟥 THE BATTLEFIELD IS NOW IN COURTROOMS

Butuyan ended with something that should resonate with every Filipino who claims to love this country: 

“Our ancestors fought in the battlefield to secure our right to life. In these modern times, the battleground for the fight to protect our right to life is within the four walls of this honorable court."

He's right. 

Our heroes died fighting colonizers and dictators so we could have the right to life, to due process, to be presumed innocent until proven guilty. 

Then we elected someone who threw all that away, who told police to kill suspects on sight, who promised pardons for murder.

The ICC confirmation hearing will determine whether Rodrigo Duterte faces trial. 

But underneath all the legal procedures, something bigger is at stake: whether we as Filipinos still value the rule of law, or if we’ve permanently exchanged it for strongman politics and summary executions.

🟥 THE MESSAGE WE'RE PRETENDING NOT TO HEAR

Butuyan addressed the judges, but his words were meant for Manila, for Davao, for every barangay where families still live in fear of speaking out. 

He was telling us that the "virus of impunity" didn't end when Duterte got arrested. It's still spreading. 

It's infected how we think about justice, about human rights, about the value of life itself.

The question is whether we're willing to take the cure. 

Whether we're ready to admit that cheering for extrajudicial killings made us complicit. 

Whether we can look at those 497 victim families and see ourselves in their place instead of congratulating ourselves for not being "criminals."

Because if we can't, if we let this case fail or fade into noise, then Butuyan's warning will come true. 

The virus keeps spreading. 
Another "apostle of impunity" gets elected. 
The cycle continues. 

And that island where the screams never stop? We're already living on it.

We just haven't admitted it yet.

This comment was left on my Kaufman/Panelo piece. 

I’m only pulling it out here because of the fallacies and the realities it quietly skips. 

In short, his claim is that Duterte’s case is driven by politics and that sending him to The Hague was rushed.  

If you’re an ordinary Pinoy, para matatakot ka: wow, ang advance ng reasoning niya. So tahimik ka na lang kasi mukhang magaling ito, even if the logic is already shaky.  

This is why I keep urging everyone to read and do some basic checking before reacting. 

Anyway, I got curious about the connections he was trying to draw, so I spent a bit more time looking into them.  

Here’s my response based on that research. I’m not an expert and I’m not a geopolitical analyst, but I at least want to see if his argument survives contact with the facts.

🟥 WRONG COURT, WRONG ANALOGY  

He’s using South Africa’s case against Israel in Gaza to “explain” how the ICC works, but that already breaks down at step one. 

South Africa went to the ICJ, a court where states sue other states. 

Duterte is in front of the ICC, a court that prosecutes individuals for crimes like crimes against humanity. 

Different court, different rules, different role.  

When you mix those two up and then build an argument on top of that, you’re already standing on sand. It sounds smart, but it’s a false equivalence.  

🟥 THE “POLITICAL MOTIVATION” RED HERRING  

Next move: “Political motivation is a strong counter argument.”  

Sure, politics is always in the background of international cases. But saying “political” over and over doesn’t magically erase evidence. 

The ICC only moved forward after judges looked at years of records, patterns of killings, and witness accounts and decided there were reasonable grounds to believe Duterte was responsible for crimes against humanity.  

If the defense wants to use “political motivation,” the real test is simple:  

- Did politics invent the bodies?  
- Did politics fabricate the timelines, the patterns, the public threats, the police operations?  

If you can’t show that political motive changed the facts, then “politics” becomes a convenient distraction. 

That’s the fallacy here: he shifts the conversation to motive and completely skips the substance of the case. 

Classic red herring.  

Even in Philippine courts, people raise political motivation all the time. And? Cases still rise and fall on evidence. Judges don’t acquit just because a defendant says, “This is political.”  

🟥 WHY THERE WAS “RUSH” TO SEND HIM TO THE HAGUE  

Now to his big question: “First why the rush to send him to the Hague?”  

Let’s lay out what he’s ignoring:  

- There was an ICC arrest warrant, based on a judicial finding that there are reasonable grounds to believe Duterte committed crimes against humanity connected to the drug war and earlier Davao killings.  

- For years, Philippine institutions refused to seriously go after the architects of the drug war. Almost all the “accountability” stayed at the level of low‑rank cops, if that.  

- The ICC only has jurisdiction when a state is unwilling or unable to genuinely investigate and prosecute. That’s exactly the situation here.  

Once he was arrested in Philippine territory under that warrant, the “rush” to send him to The Hague is not some mystery conspiracy. It’s called complying with an existing judicial process and reducing the time window for:  

- Political interference  
- Last‑minute legal stunts  
- Flight risk and “missing” accused  

If they had dragged their feet, the same people now crying “rush” would probably be cheering if he slipped away. 

The state moved quickly because, for once, there was a real chance to actually hand a powerful man over to a court that can hear the victims and weigh the evidence.  

🟥 WHAT HIS COMMENT CONVENIENTLY LEAVES OUT  

Notice what he never talks about:  

- The thousands killed in the drug war and earlier operations.  

- The near‑zero high‑level accountability in Philippine courts.  

- The years of public threats and “joke but not really” lines that created a climate where killings were rewarded, not punished.  

Instead, he jumps straight to “political motivation” and “why the rush,” as if Duterte was minding his own business and suddenly got picked up as a favor to some foreign agenda. 

No mention of the victims. 
No mention of the families who had to watch case after case go nowhere at home.  

So if we’re going to talk about what’s “political,” we also need to ask:  

- Was it not political when the system shielded him for years?  

- Was it not political when local cases stalled, got downgraded, or never even reached powerful names?  

If anything, sending Duterte to The Hague is the first time the politics finally bent, even a little, toward people who have been screaming for justice since the first bodies started piling up.

🟥 PANELO’S LOW BAR FOR “GOOD LAWYERING”  

Panelo praising Kaufman’s performance says more about Panelo’s standards than Kaufman’s lawyering. 

When a former chief presidential legal counsel looks at that opening statement and calls it a good job, what he’s really revealing is how broken the Duterte camp’s expectations of “defense” have become. 

Instead of asking whether Kaufman actually dented the prosecution’s case, Panelo is cheering like a fan reacting to a tribute video.  

Kaufman spent his precious time in front of ICC judges turning Duterte into “the people’s president” and “Tatay Digong,” insisting the prosecutors just didn’t “get” his hyperbole. 

He painted the issue as the ICC being unfair to a misunderstood, joke‑cracking old man, while the court is looking at systematic killings and coordinated operations, not fan‑service speeches. 

If Panelo thinks that’s “doing well,” then of course he’ll say they’re on the right track: he’s grading on loyalty, not impact.  

🟥 A DEFENSE BUILT ON EMPTY SHOW  

Look at the substance of Kaufman’s argument. He took 55 speeches and tried to drown the court in numbers, saying 35 talked about lawful self‑defense and only 20 were the “bad” ones the ICC chose. 

That is basic misdirection: bury the clear calls to violence under a pile of cleaner quotes, then act like the court should treat it like a scorecard.  

He barely engaged with the core allegation: that Duterte’s words linked to real police work and real bodies on the ground. 

He brushed off witnesses as having “next to zero” reliability, the way a random supporter would talk online, not counsel in a hearing on crimes against humanity. 

Then he leaned hard on a political plot story, blaming Marcos Jr. and hinting about some phone call he would not even lay out properly for the judges. 

For a mass‑killing case, it’s a joke of an answer, closer to DDS bar‑room talk than anything a serious lawyer should say.  

🟥 WHY SARA EVEN FLOATED REPLACEMENT  

Even inside their own circle, people were not blind to how weak this looked. 

Sara Duterte openly said she would talk to her father about changing his ICC lawyers after Kaufman kept failing to stop or slow the process. 

The Pre‑Trial Chamber rejected attempts to use Duterte’s health as a shield, and outside experts said he could still face the court. 

For a clan used to winning on pressure and fear, that string of failures made Kaufman look small.  

That is why Sara floated the idea of a new team in the first place: not because the ICC suddenly became fair, but because their “lead” counsel could not even buy them time. 

Duterte still chose to keep him, which shows what this whole setup really is: not a serious legal fight, but a performance meant to talk to fans.  

🟥 LOSING IN COURT, PRAISED IN PUBLIC  

Outside the ICC hearing room, the pattern is the same. 

Malacañang had already told Duterte’s legal camp to “do better” after earlier weak moves; even Marcos Jr.’s own people saw that the work coming from that side was underwhelming. 

Kaufman’s filings did not change the path of the case: the court moved ahead, the confirmation‑of‑charges hearing opened, and the judges pushed aside most of the stalling tricks.  

Panelo skips all of that and clings to the show, because once he admits the defense is weak, he has to admit that years of tough‑guy posturing have left Duterte exposed when the rules are real. 

So instead, he claps for a speech that did nothing for Duterte where it counts and pretends that is enough.  

🟥 DDS TALKING POINTS IN A REAL COURTROOM  

Kaufman’s main points could have been lifted from any loud DDS page:  

- Duterte is just being honest and exaggerating.  
- The ICC is cherry‑picking what to quote.  
- The real enemy is a supposed political plot from Marcos Jr. and his camp.  

None of that answers the key questions about command responsibility, police work, or the record on killings. 

It is a set of talking points trying to act like legal analysis, and the ICC has seen that kind of move before from other strongman regimes.  

So when Panelo praises him, it sounds less like a lawyer speaking and more like one long‑time loyalist recognizing another. 

The court is weighing documents and human lives; Kaufman is selling a story his crowd likes to hear.  

🟥 WHEN “GOOD JOB” MEANS THE BAR IS ON THE FLOOR  

If the standard is “he defended Tatay Digong with emotion” instead of “he managed to shake the evidence,” then yes, Panelo can say Kaufman did his job.

I hate writing this blog.

Not because it’s hard—Philippine politics always gives us material—but because this is not the outcome I want. 

I didn’t sit down with my coffee hoping to conclude that the most serious charges against the sitting vice president will probably be decided not in the Senate or the courts, but at the ballot box in 2028. 

I started this because I kept seeing posts on my feed—“she’s finished,” “the Senate will convict,” “this is her karma”—and I wanted to see if the facts actually support that hope.

When you strip away the law, the numbers, and the current Senate, a harsh picture appears: impeachment looks more like a process that will be completed for formality than a battle that will be won. 

But that doesn’t mean Sara Duterte is inevitable.

I also don’t want to feed the myth that she’s inevitable. 

Rep. Benny Abante Jr. put it well: she may be “winnable,” but she’s not “unbeatable.” 

That line is important if we’re going to talk honestly about where the real fight moves.

This article comes with a prayer: that I am wrong. 

That, in the name of the Philippines, a miracle happens to spare us from the hands of another Duterte. 

Because to this day, we are still recovering from the institutional damage left by Rodrigo Duterte—and in the hands of a low‑quality politician like Sara Duterte, who has shown zero real leadership and management skill, and whose main interest appears to be protecting her family name, this already downtrodden country risks being pushed even further down into the mud.

This is my attempt to understand the issue better, not to surrender to it.

🟥 THE HARD RULE: 16 OF 24

Everything starts with one constitutional rule we usually only look up when a crisis hits.

The impeachment provisions says that no impeached official is convicted without the vote of two‑thirds of all the Members of the Senate. 

In a 24‑member Senate, that means 16 votes to convict—and that denominator does not shrink just because some senators are absent or abstain.

That’s the part I kept trying to poke holes in. 

Maybe it’s two‑thirds of those present? 

Maybe jurisprudence quietly adjusted it? 

But the standard legal and academic explainers all line up on the same answer: conviction requires 16, not “16 if everyone shows up, less if they don’t.”

And if you need 16 to convict, you only need 9 to block conviction.

That’s the asymmetry at the heart of this whole story: the defense doesn’t need a majority; it only needs a solid minority.

🟥 THE 20TH CONGRESS: NEW SENATE, SAME INSTINCT

We’re not guessing based on some old Senate line‑up. The Senate that will sit as an impeachment court now is the 20th Congress, elected after the 2025 midterms.

That matters because:

- The 2025 midterms strengthened a Duterte‑aligned bloc in the Senate.

- Leadership and committee assignments may shift, but political loyalties don’t evaporate overnight.

- Most importantly: this same Senate has already been tested.

In August 2025, the Senate voted to archive the articles of impeachment against Sara Duterte 19–4–1. 

Nineteen senators voted to put the case away. 
Four opposed. 
One abstained.

That vote isn’t the same as a “guilty/not guilty” verdict, but it is a clear signal: when this chamber was asked whether to keep walking toward a possible conviction, it chose not to—by a wide margin.

If you’re trying to find 16 conviction votes, you’re not starting from neutral ground. 

You’re starting in a chamber that has already shown a strong instinct to avoid taking Sara all the way to judgment.

🟥 THE SHIELD: WHY THE 2028 ANNOUNCEMENT MATTERS

Here’s the part that feels very Filipino: once Sara says she’s running for president, the impeachment vote no longer just becomes the issue about a vice president on trial. 

It becomes a rehearsal for 2028 alliances.

If you’re a senator with your own ambitions, you now have to ask:

- Am I willing to be one of 16 who voted to convict a woman who might sit in Malacañang two years from now?

- Or do I take the safer route: respect the process, maybe criticize her publicly, but ultimately not supply a conviction vote—and say, “Let the people decide in 2028”?

Her early announcement raises the political cost of voting “guilty” without changing the legal threshold. The bar is still 16, but the risk for each “yes” vote increased.

So the announcement doesn’t create the numbers. It just makes the numbers harder to challenge. 

🟥 “WINNABLE, NOT UNBEATABLE”: WHY THAT LINE IS CRITICAL

This is where Benny Abante’s framing becomes important.

He’s not sugarcoating Sara’s strength. He acknowledges she can look “winnable” at this point. 

But he insists that winnable does not mean unbeatable, and that there is still a long stretch between now and 2028 where many things can still happen.

He also argues that her early declaration could actually work against her in the long run, precisely because it forces her into the spotlight earlier than she might like: more time for questions about the impeachment allegations, more time for unresolved issues to be probed on the campaign trail, more time for voters to weigh integrity against branding.

To me, this is the mental reset we need. 

The Senate math may seal the impeachment’s fate, but it doesn’t seal the election. 

If accountability can’t land in the impeachment court, it has to migrate into the political arena. 

That’s not ideal. But pretending it’s impossible is exactly how dynasties become inevitable.

🟥 THE MESSY REALITY: SCANDALS, ICC EXPOSURE, AND MOVING PIECES

On one side, there is the flood control scam issue. 

Certain senators previously seen as friendly to archiving or soft‑landing Sara’s case have been linked in public reporting to allegations around flood control anomalies, kickbacks, and recommended charges to the Ombudsman. 

Even if not yet final, this changes incentives and makes the ground shaky. 

On another front, internationally, ICC-related filings and narratives have dragged in the names of key Duterte-aligned senators (especially those historically tied to the drug war period). 

Whether or not anything immediately happens, the political and legal pressure is real—and it makes the landscape less stable.

All of this introduces volatility. Some senators who previously looked like safe votes may soon be fighting their own legal fires. 

In a worst-case scenario for Sara, you could imagine:

- Certain senators sidelined or more hesitant to be seen openly shielding her.

- Others recalculating because the public mood shifts or because legal pressure becomes personal.

It’s important to acknowledge that. But when you strip things down to the hard floor instead of the full wish list, the picture is still sobering:

Even if you mentally remove the scandal-tainted names from her column and assume they are unavailable or flip against her, the core Duterte-aligned group can still plausibly hold enough votes to form the blocking minority needed to prevent 16 conviction votes.

So yes, the scandals and ICC exposure make the map uglier and less predictable. They damage the edges of her shield. But they don’t remove the shield itself.

🟥 SO WHAT ACTUALLY KICKS HER OUT—AS VP AND AS A 2028 CANDIDATE?

If impeachment conviction is structurally unlikely, what can actually remove Sara Duterte from office and bar her from 2028?

Think of five routes:

1) SENATE CONVICTION

The clean, constitutional way: 16 senators vote “guilty” on at least one article. She is removed and, under the usual understanding of impeachment penalties, barred from future office.

The law allows it. The numbers don’t—at least not as things stand.

2) FINAL CRIMINAL CONVICTION WITH DISQUALIFYING PENALTIES

Separate from impeachment, plunder and related complaints tied to the confidential funds issue have been filed and discussed publicly. If these cases reach trial and end in a final conviction with penalties that include disqualification, she can be forced out of office and kept off the ballot.

But the crucial question is timing. Our justice system is slow. Elections come fast. If cases don’t reach finality before the 2028 filing and campaign window closes in, this route becomes less about preventing candidacy and more about chasing it.

3) COMELEC DISQUALIFICATION

COMELEC can disqualify candidates on certain statutory grounds, often tied to final convictions. This route typically depends on the criminal route producing a final judgment.

4) RESIGNATION

She could resign as vice president and still run for president later, unless disqualified by conviction. Some see resignation as a way to “preempt” harsher consequences.

But based on how she’s moving—declaring early, recruiting, consolidating—she has little incentive to resign now.

5) LOSING IN 2028

If she isn’t removed by impeachment and isn’t legally disqualified, the only institution left that can stop her is not the Senate. It’s the electorate.

That’s not satisfying if what you want is legal accountability now. But it is the remaining route if institutions move slowly or shrink away.

🟥 THE WAY I SEE IT NOW: IMPEACHMENT IS FORMALITY, CASES ARE PROCESS, THE REAL BATTLE IS 2028

So this is where all of that brings me, over too many cups of coffee.

The impeachment—if it happens—looks like a formality in the sense that the outcome is structurally tilted by the 16‑vote rule and by the Senate’s revealed posture in the previous cycle. 

It can still be important as a record, as public airing, as institutional assertion. But “important” doesn’t automatically mean “effective.”

The cases filed are also process. Real process. Necessary process. But likely slow process. And in a country where calendars decide political fate, slow process often becomes a polite way to postpone accountability.

So yes: the real battle will be in 2028.

That doesn’t mean it’s hopeless. It means the battlefield changes.

This is why I see Abante’s framing as important. If Sara is “winnable but not unbeatable,” then the job of those who want accountability is not to pray for a sudden 16‑vote Senate conversion. It’s to build what makes her beatable:

- sustained scrutiny,
- clarity on alternatives,
- opposition unity,
- and a campaign that turns “questions” into “answers demanded.”

Because if the legal routes stall, then the political route is not optional—it becomes the only route that still has a realistic clock.

🟥 CONCLUSION: A PRAYER TO BE PROVEN WRONG

So here’s where I land, and I say this with a heavy heart:

- The Constitution sets a high bar—16 of 24 senators to convict, 9 to block—and this Senate has already shown it leans toward blocking.

- The core Duterte bloc is large enough, even with scandals and ICC clouds, to keep impeachment from reaching 16 “guilty” votes.

- The cases against her are real and serious, but the clock of Philippine justice runs much slower than the calendar of a presidential campaign.

- Her 2028 declaration turned impeachment from a legal proceeding into the opening battle of a presidential race, making senators even more cautious.

Impeachment and cases, as things stand, look more like process than solution.

This piece comes with a prayer that I am wrong. That, for once, institutions surprise us. That in the name of the Philippines, something breaks the pattern and spares us from sliding into the hands of another Duterte.

We are still, even now, recovering from the institutional damage left by Rodrigo Duterte. In the hands of a low‑quality politician like Sara Duterte—who has shown no serious leadership or management skill, and whose main visible project is to protect her family name—that damage wouldn’t just remain. It would deepen. A country already down in the mud can always be pushed lower.

And if I’m being honest, I’m also writing this for one more reason: to be proven wrong.

I want someone with real authority—someone who can actually move institutions, not just explain them—to read this and say: “No, Gino. You’re wrong. We will do everything in our power to make her accountable.” Not in slogans. Not in press statements. In actions that finally match the gravity of what’s at stake.

Because the longer this drags on, the more familiar it feels: the hearings, the filings, the denunciations, the counter‑narratives—and then the quiet settling back into normal, as if the country didn’t just absorb another blow.

Maybe that’s why this piece sounds like it does. Not because I want to sound defeated, but because I’ve watched this pattern repeat enough times to recognize the shape of it before it ends.

So yes—impeachment and cases may be the process. 2028 may be the verdict. But I’m still hoping to be interrupted by something rarer than a clever strategy or a strong survey number: a public institution that finally decides it will not blink.

Please help me pray.

🟥 WHILE YOU'RE CELEBRATING FAKE ACHIEVEMENTS, REAL CORRUPTION IS HAPPENING

There's an infographic making the rounds. Vice President Sara Duterte's "achievements." Big numbers. Slick design. A 98% satisfaction rating stamped at the bottom like a seal of approval.

P612.5 million in confidential funds with fake receipts.

House investigators found that around 60% of the people listed as confidential fund recipients don't exist in the civil registry. "Mary Grace Piattos" has a P1 million reward for anyone who can find her. No birth certificate. No records. Nothing.

The Commission on Audit flagged the OVP for disbursing P125 million in confidential funds in just 11 days—December 19 to 29, 2022. The liquidation documents had names like "Kokoy Villamin" and food items like "Kamote" and "Mango" listed as supposed recipients.

These patterns point to a deliberate scheme, not harmless errors.

P53 million a year in office rent.

Sara's OVP spends P4.4 million monthly—P53 million yearly—on satellite office leases across the country. Lawmakers called it waste of money. COA is looking into whether these offices even serve their stated purpose.

Leni Robredo's OVP spent P4.1 million total for her entire six-year term. Not per year. Total.

Sara spends 13 times more on rent alone than Leni spent in six years. For what? Offices that reportedly sit idle while confidential funds disappear into fake names.

P3.4 million in livelihood grants with no feasibility studies.

COA's 2024 audit found that the OVP released P3.4 million to livelihood beneficiaries without conducting feasibility or viability studies. No assessment of whether the projects would succeed. Just money out, no real guardrails.

That’s taxpayer money treated like campaign funds.

The programs being celebrated got defunded.

That P820 million medical and burial assistance program in the infographic? Congress cut it in 2025. No more funds allocated. The program that's supposed to prove Sara's effectiveness no longer exists because of budget issues and questions on use.

You're celebrating a program that couldn't survive the next budget cycle.

🟥 NOW LET'S TALK BUDGET

Sara Duterte's OVP received P2.084 billion in 2024.

Leni Robredo's highest single-year budget was P900 million in 2021.

Sara got more than double. Let's see what each did with their money.

Leni's P900 million:

- 87,560 individuals served through medical assistance in 2021 alone  
- About P1.15 billion total spent on medical aid over five years, or roughly P13,131 per person  
- Angat Buhay program mobilized around P520 million from private partners, reaching 622,000 families across 223 cities  
- COVID response: Swab Cab, Vaccine Express, Bayanihan E-Konsulta, community learning hubs  
- Full transparency—every expense published online with receipts  

Sara's P2.084 billion:

- 1.78 million beneficiaries served, or about P1,001 per person  
- P53 million spent on office rent alone  
- Hundreds of millions in confidential funds tied to highly dubious receipts  
- Flagship programs defunded the next year for questionable performance or priorities  
- No public breakdown of confidential fund use despite COA and congressional scrutiny  

Sara spent more money, reached more people on paper, but delivered weaker per-person aid and left huge gaps in accountability.

🟥 WHAT ABOUT THE NUMBERS IN THE INFOGRAPHIC

Let's be specific about what that graphic is celebrating.

1.78 million clients served (76% over target)

The Philippines has 117 million people. That’s roughly 1.5% of the population.

Also: who set the target? The OVP sets its own targets. If you aim low, beating your goal is easy.

P820 million in medical and burial aid to 187,297 clients

That's about P4,379 per person. Not nothing, but much lower than what Leni’s office delivered per person in medical assistance with a smaller budget.

And that program no longer exists. It was defunded in 2025.

1.02 million passengers in Libreng Sakay

The 1.02 million figure is real, but zoom in on implementation. In Cebu—the pilot in the Visayas—only about half of the 100 assigned buses consistently showed up. Traditional jeepney drivers protested the scheme as unfair since big fleet operators got the subsidies. Transport advocates called it unsustainable and a temporary fix instead of real transport reform.

144,808 rice food packs distributed nationwide

There are around 24 million poor families in the country.

144,808 packs reach roughly 0.6% of them.

PanSarap: 427,958 buns to 14,436 learners

There are about 28 million students in the Philippines.

Fourteen thousand four hundred thirty-six is around 0.05%.

At that scale, you're not changing a national education reality. You're touching a few pockets of kids and getting a lot of photos.

MagNegosyo Ta' Day: assistance to 1,003 Filipinos and 16 small businesses

There are about 1 million MSMEs in the Philippines.

Sixteen businesses barely show up on a graph.

🟥 THE 98% SATISFACTION RATING MEANS VERY LITTLE

That 98% satisfaction rating tells you people are grateful they got help, not whether the program is well-designed or sufficient.

If you're drowning and someone throws you a rope, you're grateful. That gratitude doesn't prove the entire rescue system works; it only proves you survived.

ISO 9001 certification means processes are documented and consistently followed. It doesn't measure whether those processes produce deep, lasting impact—or whether they quietly enable abuse somewhere else in the system.

You can have neat paperwork and still hide a lot of dirt.

🟥 WHAT THIS INFOGRAPHIC IS REALLY DOING

Sara didn't personally post this infographic; the DDS troll network did. This is propaganda work, not neutral reporting.

They're banking on you not knowing about Mary Grace Piattos.

They're banking on you not asking why Congress slashed her 2025 budget from P2 billion to P733 million and reallocated P1.3 billion to social and health programs.

They're banking on you missing the fact that Sara refused to defend her budget in Congress, skipping the hearing and sending a letter saying she wouldn't participate.

They're banking on you seeing "1.78 million served" and never wondering "served how much, compared to what, and what happened to the rest of the money?"

They're banking on you forgetting that while these issues were heating up, she was making public threats against the president, the first lady, and the House speaker—a spectacle that conveniently pulled attention away from the confidential fund mess.

Low standards are their biggest ally.

🟥 WHAT ACTUAL EXCELLENCE LOOKS LIKE

Real public service operates at scale. Not 0.05% coverage. Not 1.5% of the population. It moves millions, not thousands.

Real public service is transparent. Every peso accounted for. Receipts from real people, not made-up names. Third-party audits. Publicly accessible reports.

Real public service doesn't burn P53 million a year on rent while claiming limited resources.

Real public service doesn't rush P125 million in confidential spending through 11 days, then insist everything is aboveboard while resisting deeper scrutiny.

Real public service shows up in Congress and explains, line by line, where the money went, even when the questions are hostile. It doesn’t walk away from its own budget hearing.

Leni Robredo's OVP showed that kind of work: smaller budget, larger per-person impact, heavy reliance on partnerships instead of secret funds, and full public reporting.

Sara had the chance to meet that bar and chose a different path.

🟥 WHY THIS MATTERS

You can look at that infographic and just feel good. The numbers look big. The design is clean. It scratches that itch of "at least someone is doing something."

Or you can ask why Congress cut her budget by P1.3 billion if everything is so successful.

You can ask why COA keeps flagging her office.

You can ask why so many supposed confidential fund recipients have no records in the civil registry.

You can ask why she wouldn't sit in a hearing and defend her numbers.

You can ask why the medical aid program being celebrated in that graphic couldn't even survive to the next year.

You can ask why her office rent looks like a luxury lease compared to her predecessor.

Those questions are not bitterness and not partisan attacks. They're the minimum level of curiosity a taxpayer should have.

🟥 THIS ISN'T JUST ABOUT SARA

Sara Duterte as vice president is the example here, but the habit I’m arguing for—questioning, digging deeper, demanding receipts—has to apply to every politician.

Whether we like them or not.

Especially when we like them.

That’s usually where we fail. We tolerate the bad habits of politicians we support. We ignore red flags. We say "lahat naman corrupt" or "at least may nagagawa" and let it slide. We share their infographics without checking the numbers.

That tolerance is on us. That blind loyalty is our weakness.

When we stop asking hard questions because the official is "ours," we help them get away with more. That makes us part of the environment that allows corruption and waste to thrive, whether we realize it or not.

The same standard you're applying to Sara right now? Use it on your mayor, your governor, your favorite senator, even your most admired reformist. Ask for their budgets, their impact, their audits, their failures.

If your politician can't face that kind of scrutiny, maybe they don't deserve your defense.

🟥 RAISING THE STANDARD

Next time you see a government accomplishment post, try this:

- Ask what the budget was and compute how much was spent per person.  
- Ask what percentage of the target population was reached.  
- Ask how it compares to previous officials who had less money.  
- Ask what changed long term, not just who got a one-time handout.  
- Ask which programs were quietly dropped or defunded.  
- Ask if the recipients in the documents are real people you can actually find.  
- Ask why budgets get slashed if performance is supposedly excellent.  
- Ask why some officials are afraid of budget hearings.

Those questions are not unpatriotic. They’re the point of having a democracy in the first place.

The bar right now is on the floor. It’s been down there so long that a glossy infographic can look like proof of greatness.

Sara Duterte's "accomplishments" show what happens when a country stops demanding excellence and starts applauding bare-minimum performance disguised with design and PR.

The numbers in that graphic are real. The programs existed. Some people genuinely received help.

But while you're counting rice packs and buns, hundreds of millions moved through fake names and untraceable spending.

If we don't learn to question that, we’re not just victims of the trick.

We’re part of why it works.

Did Bong Go just confirm what the ICC has been saying all along—that the killings were state-sponsored?

[An MCT Commentary]

"Walang iniutos si FPRRD sa akin na iligal." Former President Rodrigo Duterte never gave him illegal orders. He said this defending himself. But I kept thinking—he's technically right. That's exactly the problem.

Go thinks he's clearing his name. But he's actually confirming what the International Criminal Court has been arguing since 2021. The killings weren't rogue operations. They were state policy. 

When murder becomes policy, you don't need illegal orders.

🟥 When State Policy Makes Murder Legal

Here's what caught my attention about Go's defense. He's not denying the killings happened. He's not denying he had a role. He's saying the orders were legal. 

And that's precisely what the ICC is prosecuting.

The ICC defines crimes against humanity as acts committed "pursuant to or in furtherance of a State or organizational policy to commit such attack" against a civilian population. Not whether individual orders were legal within the system, but whether the system itself was designed to kill.

Go's defense fits that definition perfectly. 

Yes, the orders came through proper channels. Yes, the chain of command was clear. Yes, the budget was approved. Yes, Duterte authorized it. He's not denying any of this. 

He's confirming it. And calling it legal.

The ICC Pre-Trial Chamber already ruled that "the so-called 'war on drugs' campaign cannot be seen as a legitimate law enforcement operation, and the killings neither as legitimate nor as mere excesses in an otherwise legitimate operation."

When Go says Duterte never ordered him to do anything illegal, he's revealing how the system worked. 

You don't order someone to commit murder when murder is the stated goal. You give them authority, budget, coordination responsibilities. 

You build rewards systems. You protect perpetrators from prosecution. You make the killing routine.

🟥 The Structure Of Sanctioned Killing

The ICC's Document Containing the Charges against Duterte, released February 13, names Go as one of eight co-perpetrators. 

According to multiple reports on the filing, from 1998 to 2016, Go served as Duterte's executive assistant in Davao City. 

From June 2016 to October 2018, he was Special Assistant to the President. 

The prosecution alleges Go had authority over the Davao Death Squad alongside Senator Bato dela Rosa, Vicente Danao, and Sonny Buenaventura.

The hierarchy prosecutors describe is clean. Methodical. 

At the bottom were DDS members—hitmen and lower-ranking officers who executed the killings. 

Above them were handlers—police or barangay officials who managed the shooters. 

Those handlers reported upward to police leadership and co-perpetrators. 

Go was in that coordination layer, where orders came down and money went up.

Arturo Lascañas, a self-confessed DDS member whose 186-page affidavit is now part of the ICC record, said Go entered the picture in the late 1990s. 

“Later on, orders to kill were relayed through Bong Go because he was allowed to organize his own death squad." 

That squad was allegedly led by Royina Garma and Edilberto Leonardo.

Lascañas said the money from Mayor Duterte would reach Go in millions every month. 

Sometimes Lascañas and Buenaventura delivered that money to Garma's office to finance operations. 

Go managed a rewards system. Police got 20,000 to 100,000 pesos per hit depending on the target's importance.

Everything budgeted. Everything rewarded. Everything documented.

🟥 Administrative Role, Operational Murder

Go now says his role was "administrative and clerical in nature." 

He managed Duterte's personal concerns, not police operations. He had no operational authority.

I keep thinking about this defense. 

Because administrative roles in government enable operational outcomes. 

The Special Assistant to the President oversees internal management, coordinates with presidential staff, handles appointments, manages information flow. 

It sits between policy and execution.

If Go coordinated rewards, transmitted approvals, managed budgets for killings—and the ICC filing alleges he did—then he weaponized that administrative position for murder. 

You don't need to be a police chief to run a death squad if you control the budget and the communication lines.

The ICC doesn't claim Go personally killed anyone. They claim he facilitated. He organized. He coordinated. He transmitted Duterte's sanction, which prosecutors say was necessary for DDS members to carry out hits in Davao City.

When Duterte became president in 2016, the system went national. 

What worked in Davao became the template for the entire country. 

Police got financial incentives—50,000 to one million pesos for high-value targets. Officers planted weapons and drugs on victims to justify the kills.

Go says he just followed orders that were "naaayon sa batas ng tao at batas ng Diyos." According to the law of man and the law of God.

But I want to ask him: whose law? Duterte's law, where killing suspected drug users was policy? Or actual law, where extrajudicial execution is murder?

🟥 The Numbers Behind The Policy

Official Philippine National Police figures admit to 6,252 killings during anti-drug operations between July 1, 2016 and May 31, 2022. 

Human Rights Watch documented police falsifying evidence. 

The UN calculated at least 8,663 deaths. The Philippine Commission on Human Rights estimates the real number could be triple that.

A 2021 University of the Philippines study found at least two people killed daily since the drug war began. 2016, 2019, and 2021 were the deadliest years, averaging over two to three killings per day.

I keep coming back to these numbers. 

Thousands dead. 

Most from poor urban communities. Bodies with cardboard signs reading "pusher." No due process. No trials. Just execution.

Senator Antonio Trillanes said it in 2018: "They basically admitted that there are no so-called vigilante killings—that these deaths are actually state-sponsored executions." 

The government's own "accomplishment reports" listed 20,322 deaths connected to the drug war through 2017.

Accomplishments.

🟥 The Legal Framework

The Rome Statute, which the Philippines ratified in 2011, requires that crimes against humanity be committed "pursuant to or in furtherance of a State or organizational policy." 

It doesn't matter if the orders were legal within that state's system. What matters is that the state designed a policy to attack civilians.

The ICC retains jurisdiction over crimes committed from November 1, 2011 to March 16, 2019, when the Philippines' withdrawal from the Rome Statute took effect. 

That window covers Duterte's final years as Davao mayor and most of his presidency. 

It covers the period when Go was allegedly coordinating the DDS and managing rewards.

Senator Antonio Trillanes submitted transcripts from Senate hearings on the drug war to the ICC. 

Those hearings included testimony about the chain of command. Duterte at the apex, trusted insiders like Go in the coordination layer, handlers managing shooters, DDS members executing.

Go's defense seems to prove the prosecution's case. 

He's saying yes, there was a policy. Yes, it came from the top. Yes, it was implemented through official channels. Yes, he played a coordinating role. Yes, it was all sanctioned.

🟥 Compassion For Whom

Go says he will continue serving with "malasakit." Compassion.

I want to know: for whom?

Not the families of Kian delos Santos, a 17-year-old shot in a Caloocan alley. 

Not the relatives of the 19 victims killed in Davao between 2013 and 2016, named in ICC count one. 

Not the 14 "high-value targets" murdered during Duterte's presidency, listed in count two. 

Not the victims of barangay clearance operations, documented in count three.

The confirmation of charges hearing is set for February 23, 2026. 

Duterte, already in ICC custody following his March 2025 arrest, will face judges who will determine if there's sufficient evidence to proceed to trial. 

The prosecution has named Go, Dela Rosa, former Justice Secretary Vitaliano Aguirre II, and five other high-ranking officials as co-perpetrators.

Go disputes the allegations and calls them "entirely unfounded." 

But the prosecutors have receipts. Testimonies. Affidavits. Government documents listing deaths as accomplishments. Kill orders documented by self-confessed hitmen. Money trails. A hierarchical structure with Duterte at the top and Go in the coordination layer.

🟥 The Defense That Convicts

Here's what Go doesn't understand. When he says Duterte never gave him illegal orders, he's not denying the ICC's allegations. He's confirming them.

The ICC isn't arguing that Duterte's inner circle broke Philippine law while carrying out some legitimate operation. They're arguing that the operation itself was a crime against humanity. That it was state-sponsored murder disguised as law enforcement.

Go saying the orders were legal just proves the legality was built into the killing machine.

Bong Go is right about one thing. Rodrigo Duterte never gave him illegal orders. But that's because the entire system was designed to make those killings legal within Duterte's Philippines.

When the state itself becomes the killer, legality becomes complicity. Go might think his defense protects him.

It convicts him.

Bam Aquino’s “PH courts, not ICC” quote is doing the rounds — but a lot of posts are stripping out the key context.

Here’s what he actually said in that interview: he believes cases linked to alleged EJKs in Duterte’s drug war should *ideally* be tried in Philippine courts, because the victims, families, and communities are all here, and justice is more meaningful where the crimes happened.

He said this in the context of the ICC Office of the Prosecutor naming Senators Bong Go and Bato dela Rosa as alleged co-perpetrators in the crimes against humanity case against Duterte. He was not saying “wala nang pakialam ang ICC” or that the ICC has no jurisdiction; he was making a point about where justice *should* be happening, in an ideal world.

The problem is that some pages and memes are using the headline “Aquino: Drug war cases should be tried in PH courts, not ICC” without the word “ideally” and without the part where he acknowledges the ongoing ICC process. Technically, the quote exists — but the way it’s being framed makes it sound like he’s echoing the usual anti-ICC line, which is misleading.

As always, context is everything. Before we react, it helps to ask: ano ba talaga ang buong pahayag, at sino ang may interes sa pagputol ng isang sentence to serve a specific spin?
